unit.name: Learning directly on point sets and learned surface parameterizations
unit.description: Introduces PointNet, point-set loss functions, AtlasNet, and the conceptual move toward deep implicit shape functions.
unit.summary: This segment shifts from voxel-centric learning to methods designed around better geometric representations. PointNet is presented as the first major network for point clouds, with the key design constraints that the model must be permutation invariant to point ordering and robust to sampling variation across the surface [ts=2783s][ts=2797s][ts=2854s][ts=2858s]. The core solution is strikingly simple: compute per-point embeddings and aggregate them with a symmetric function such as max or sum, then use the pooled representation for recognition or downstream tasks [ts=2875s][ts=2896s]. For generation, Wu explains that point outputs require set-based distances rather than pixelwise losses, highlighting Chamfer distance and Earth Mover distance as differentiable objectives for comparing unordered point clouds [ts=3022s][ts=3051s]. He then moves to AtlasNet, where an MLP learns a mapping from low-dimensional UV coordinates to 3D space, effectively learning parametric surface patches that yield smoother outputs than voxels or raw point clouds [ts=3118s][ts=3148s][ts=3231s]. The unit closes with the insight that deep networks themselves can serve as implicit functions over x,y,z queries, motivating 2019-style deep occupancy and deep SDF methods [ts=3352s][ts=3386s].

key_points.text: PointNet directly processes point clouds and must be permutation invariant because point sets are unordered.
Point-cloud models should also be sampling invariant so predictions reflect geometry rather than sampling artifacts.
PointNet aggregates per-point embeddings with a symmetric operator such as max or sum.
Chamfer distance compares two point sets by nearest-neighbor matching in both directions.
Earth Mover distance uses a one-to-one bipartite matching between point sets.
AtlasNet learns an MLP mapping from UV coordinates to 3D surface points, using multiple sheets for complex shapes.
Deep implicit functions query x,y,z with a network to predict occupancy or signed distance directly.

kp_catalog.name: PointNet and point-set invariances
kp_catalog.description: The idea that point-cloud networks should respect permutation and sampling invariance, implemented by symmetric aggregation over point features.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because pointNet and point-set invariances formalizes how the idea that point-cloud networks should respect permutation and sampling invariance, implemented by symmetric aggregation over point features, which later units use as core machinery rather than background context.

kp_catalog.name: AtlasNet and deep implicit shape decoders
kp_catalog.description: Learned mappings from low-dimensional parameter spaces or x,y,z queries to geometry, covering both patch-based surface decoders and occupancy/SDF-style networks.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because atlasNet and deep implicit shape decoders synthesizes learned mappings from low-dimensional parameter spaces or x,y,z queries to geometry, covering both patch-based surface decoders and occupancy/SDF-style networks into an end-to-end method or integrated recipe.

kp_catalog.name: Parametric curves and surfaces
kp_catalog.description: Representations that map a low-dimensional parameter domain such as t or (u,v) into points on a 2D or 3D shape.
kp_catalog.importance_rationale: Important because parametric curves and surfaces clarifies representations that map a low-dimensional parameter domain such as t or (u,v) into points on a 2D or 3D shape, sharpening later modeling, analysis, or evaluation choices.
